claims.claim: A partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (bag volumes of 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (bag volume of 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject a two-car garage door.
claims.quote: a partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject the two-car garage door.

claims.claim: The synTREG volumes sufficient to eject the garage door correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, whereas 25% of the well-mixed LFL is typically used when assessing explosion hazard precipitation.
claims.quote: These synTREG volumes correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, where 25% of the well-mixed LFL is used when assessing the precipitation of an explosion hazard.

claims.claim: Significant structural damage including cracked wooden wall studs, dislodged drywall, deformed man-door latch plates, and ejected garage doors was observed at peak internal pressures of approximately 2.51–2.79 kPa, which are approximately 50% below the Jarrett and Baker thresholds for minor structural damage.
claims.quote: The 826 L NCA and 320 L LFP gas bag experiments achieved peak internal pressures of 2.79 kPa and 2.51 kPa, respectively. In both cases, the maximum pressure was approximately 50% less than the threshold established by Jarrett or Baker.

claims.claim: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas, which is attributed to the higher burning velocity of the LFP gas.
claims.quote: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas. This is expected due to the higher burning velocity of the gas.

claims.claim: In each bag experiment case considered, the Mulpuru vented explosion model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise compared to experimental results, indicating it may provide conservative estimates for explosion hazard design calculations.
claims.quote: In each bag case considered, the model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise when compared to experimental results. This demonstrates that the Mulpuru model may provide more conservative estimates of the rate of pressure rise for explosion hazard design calculations.

claims.claim: For the 3302 L NCA gas bag experiment, the maximum overpressure was 4.12 kPa, which was lower than the 6.25 kPa measured in the 1651 L gas bag experiment, with the decrease attributed to early pressure relief through the garage door acting as a deflagration vent.
claims.quote: the largest tested bag volume of 3302 L deviates from the model trend, having a maximum overpressure of 4.12 kPa compared to 6.25 kPa for the 1651 L gas bag. This behavior may be attributed to the low-strength garage door, which relieved pressure and vented the still-combusting gas cloud, similar to a deflagration panel.
